title: Subject and Predicate
description: Learn the two core parts of a nominal sentence in Arabic: the subject (Mubtada) and the predicate (Khabar).

concepts.description: The topic of the sentence. It always has a dammah (ُ) or two dammahs (ٌ) at the end. It is always a noun or pronoun.
What is being said about the Mubtada. It also always has a dammah or two dammahs at the end. It tells us something about the subject.

rules: Both Mubtada and Khabar end with a dammah (ُ) or two dammahs (ٌ).
A Mubtada is always a noun (اسم) or pronoun (ضمير).
The Khabar tells us information about the Mubtada.
In Arabic, the verb 'is/are' is not written — it is implied.
If the Mubtada has alif-laam (ال), it gets a single dammah. Without alif-laam, it usually gets two dammahs (tanween).

items.answer: Muhammad (peace be upon him) is a Prophet.
The grandson is well-mannered and the grandfather is merciful.

title: Possessive Construction
description: Learn how to express possession and relationships between nouns using the Mudaaf and Mudaaf Ilayhi construction (Idaafah).

concepts.description: The possessed noun that comes first. It never has alif-laam (ال) and never takes tanween.
The possessor noun that comes second. It always ends with a kasrah (ِ) or two kasrahs (ٍ).

rules: The Mudaaf (first noun) NEVER has alif-laam (ال) AND never has tanween.
The Mudaaf Ilayhi (second noun) always ends with kasrah (ِ) or two kasrahs (ٍ).
This construction expresses 'X of Y' or 'Y's X' in English.
Multiple nouns can be chained: A of B of C.
The Mudaaf can be in any grammatical state (rafa', nasb, or jarr) — this affects only its final vowel, not its structure.

examples.english: The student's book / The book of the student
The servant of the mosque of the city

quranicExample.source: Al-Fatiha 1:1 — 'اسم' is Mudaaf, 'الله' is Mudaaf Ilayhi

items.text: The house's wall / wall of the house
The room's door / door of the room
The room's window / window of the room
The house's roof / roof of the house
The room's ceiling / ceiling of the room
The grandfather's pen / pen of the grandfather
The sun's heat / heat of the sun
Door of the house is high.
Ceiling of the room is expensive.

items.answer: The son of the man is intelligent.
The daughter of the woman is tall.
The door of the house is new.
The mother of Mahmood is short.
The book of Aishah is old.
The notebook of Aminah is new.
The house of Humaid is old.
The son of Khalidah is intelligent.
The prayer of travel is short.

title: Past Tense Verb
description: Learn to conjugate Arabic past tense verbs across all 14 forms — 3rd, 2nd, and 1st person, masculine and feminine, singular, dual, and plural.

concepts.description: An action that has already been completed. The root form (3rd person masculine singular) is the base form.
The subject is known and is performing the action.
The subject is unknown. Formed by changing the vowels of the verb.

rules: The base form of the past tense verb is the 3rd person masculine singular (هُوَ).
Suffixes are added to the base form to indicate person, gender, and number.
For passive voice, change the first vowel to dammah and the vowel before the last letter to kasrah.
The verb comes before its subject in a verbal sentence.
Feminine verbs add تْ at the end.

quranicExample.english: And Muhammad is not but a messenger.

grammarPoints.title: What is Sulasi Mujarrad? — ثُلَاثِي مُجَرَّد
grammarPoints.explanation: Every Arabic past tense verb is built on three root letters called the Sulasi Mujarrad (ثُلَاثِي مُجَرَّد). The placeholder pattern فَعَلَ (Fa-'a-la) represents these three letters: فَ (fa), عَ (ain), لَ (lam). For example, the verb كَتَبَ (kataba = he wrote) has roots ك-ت-ب.

grammarPoints.title: Three Persons in Arabic
grammarPoints.explanation: Arabic has three grammatical persons: 1) الغائب (Al-Ghaaib) — Third person: he/she/they, the one being talked ABOUT. 2) المُخَاطَب (Al-Mukhaatab) — Second person: you, the one being talked TO. 3) المُتَكَلِّم (Al-Mutakallim) — First person: I/we, the one SPEAKING.

grammarPoints.title: Third Person (الغائب) — He/She/They
grammarPoints.explanation: The 3rd person masculine starts with the base verb فَعَلَ (he). Add ا for dual (فَعَلَا — they two), add وا for plural (فَعَلُوا — they all). For feminine, add تْ to the base (فَعَلَتْ — she), add تَا for dual (فَعَلَتَا), and use فَعَلْنَ for plural (هُنَّ). Trick: dual always has an alif ا or ـا sound.

grammarPoints.title: Second Person (المُخَاطَب) — You
grammarPoints.explanation: All 2nd person forms start with the root + تَ/تِ/تُ (the 'ta' group). Masculine singular: فَعَلْتَ (anta). Feminine singular: فَعَلْتِ (anti — note the kasra under ت). Dual (same for m/f): فَعَلْتُمَا. Masculine plural: فَعَلْتُمْ. Feminine plural: فَعَلْتُنَّ. Trick: the ت is your clue — it appears in EVERY 2nd and 1st person form.

grammarPoints.title: First Person (المُتَكَلِّم) — I / We
grammarPoints.explanation: Only two forms: أَنَا (I) → فَعَلْتُ (note the damma ُ on ت). نَحْنُ (We) → فَعَلْنَا. Easy to remember: I = فَعَلْتُ (ends in ُ), We = فَعَلْنَا (ends in نَا).

grammarPoints.title: The Key Pattern — Sukoon on the Middle
grammarPoints.explanation: Notice that in 2nd and 1st person forms, the لَ becomes لْ (with sukoon/no vowel) before the suffix is added: فَعَلْتَ not فَعَلَتَ. This is a key spelling rule. Only in 3rd person masculine base form (هُوَ) does لَ keep its fatha: فَعَلَ.

conjugationChart.title: Past Tense Conjugation Chart (فَعَلَ)
conjugationChart.subtitle: Replace فَعَلَ with any 3-letter verb, e.g. كَتَبَ (wrote), ذَهَبَ (went), جَلَسَ (sat)

exercises.instruction: Write the فَعَلَ pattern form for each pronoun.

title: Verb, Subject, Object
description: Learn how to structure a complete verbal sentence with a verb, a subject (Fa'il), and an object (Maf'ool).

concepts.description: The action being performed. In Arabic, the verb comes FIRST in a verbal sentence.
The person or thing performing the action. Always ends with dammah (ُ) or two dammahs (ٌ). Comes second.
The person or thing to which the action is done. Always ends with fathah (َ) or two fathahs (ً). Comes third.

rules: Arabic verbal sentence order: Verb → Fa'il → Maf'ool (opposite to English).
The Fa'il always ends with dammah (ُ) or two dammahs (ٌ) — it is in the rafa' state.
The Maf'ool always ends with fathah (َ) or two fathahs (ً) — it is in the nasb state.
If a word has alif-laam (ال), it takes single dammah/fathah; without it, two dammahs/fathahs (tanween).
Mudaaf-Mudaaf Ilayhi combinations can serve as Fa'il or Maf'ool.

examples.english: Mahmood's servant opened the door of the house.

quranicExample.english: Allah has decreed: 'I will surely overcome...'

title: Prepositions
description: Learn the Arabic prepositions (huroof jaarra) that cause the following noun to take a kasrah ending.

concepts.description: Letters/words that cause the noun following them to become majroor (ending with kasrah or two kasrahs). Together called Jar-Majroor.
The noun following a preposition. It always ends with kasrah (ِ) or two kasrahs (ٍ).

rules: Any noun following a preposition becomes Majroor (ends with kasrah ِ or ٍ).
If the noun has alif-laam (ال), it gets single kasrah; without it, two kasrahs.
The preposition + noun together form a Jar-Majroor phrase.
Jar-Majroor can function as a Khabar in a nominal sentence.
Some prepositions attach directly to the following word (like بِ and لِ).

examples.english: The book is on the table.
The man went to the mosque.

quranicExample.english: All praise is for Allah, the Lord of all worlds.
quranicExample.source: Surah Al-Fatiha 1:2 — 'لِ' is the preposition, 'لَّهِ' is majroor

title: Pronouns
description: Learn both detached (munfasil) and attached (muttasil) pronouns in Arabic for all persons, genders, and numbers.

concepts.description: Standalone pronouns that appear as separate words (e.g., هُوَ, هِيَ, أَنَا).
Pronouns that attach to the end of nouns, verbs, or prepositions (e.g., كِتَابُهُ = his book).

rules: Detached pronouns are used as Mubtada or to bring emphasis.
Attached pronouns attach to nouns (showing possession), verbs (as object), or prepositions.
When attached to a noun, they function like Mudaaf Ilayhi (causing the noun to lose tanween).
Arabic has separate pronouns for masculine/feminine and singular/dual/plural — 14 pronouns total.
First and second person pronouns are gender-neutral in the first person.

quranicExample.english: You alone we worship, and You alone we ask for help.
quranicExample.source: Surah Al-Fatiha 1:5 — إِيَّاكَ is an emphatic detached pronoun

title: Present & Future Tense Verb
description: Learn how to conjugate and use present/future tense verbs in Arabic, including active and passive voice forms.

concepts.description: Verbs expressing ongoing or future actions. Formed by adding prefixes (and sometimes suffixes) to the root.

rules: Present tense verbs always begin with one of four prefixes: أَ (ana), نَ (nahnu), يَ (huwa/hum), تَ (anta/hiya).
Mnemonic for the four prefixes: أَنَيْتُ (I came).
For passive voice, change the prefix vowel to dammah and the vowel before the last letter to fathah.
The present tense can refer to actions happening now OR actions that will happen in the future.
To specify future, add سَ or سَوْفَ before the verb.

examples.english: The man will write a letter.

quranicExample.arabic: وَاللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ
quranicExample.transliteration: wa-llāhu yarzuqu man yashāʾu bi-ghayri ḥisābin
quranicExample.english: And Allah provides for whom He wills without account.
quranicExample.source: Surah Al-Baqarah 2:212 — يَرْزُقُ and يَشَاءُ are present tense verbs

title: Adjective
description: Learn how adjectives work in Arabic — they follow the noun they describe and must agree in gender, number, and definiteness.

concepts.description: Describes the noun. In Arabic, adjectives come AFTER the noun they describe.
The noun being described. The adjective must agree with it in gender, number, and definiteness.

rules: The adjective (Sifa) ALWAYS comes AFTER the noun it describes.
Both noun and adjective must agree in gender (masculine/feminine) and number.
Both must agree in definiteness: both have alif-laam (ال) or both are indefinite.
Both take the same harakah at the end (dammah, fathah, or kasrah).
Three grammatical states: Rafa' (ُ/ٌ), Nasb (َ/ً), Jarr (ِ/ٍ).

quranicExample.source: Surah Al-Fatiha 1:6 — الصِّرَاطَ (Mawsoof) + الْمُسْتَقِيمَ (Sifa), both in nasb state

title: Imperative & Prohibitive
description: Learn how to form command (Amr) and prohibition (Nahy) forms from present tense verbs.

concepts.description: A command telling someone to do something. Derived from the 2nd person present tense.
A prohibition telling someone NOT to do something. Formed with لَا + jazm (sukoon) form of verb.

rules: To form the imperative: take the 2nd person present tense → remove the prefix تَ → apply sukoon to last letter → add hamza if needed.
If removing the prefix leaves a sukoon on the first letter, add a hamza (أَ, إِ, or أُ) at the beginning.
To form the prohibitive: add لَا before the 2nd person present tense verb in the jazm (sukoon) form.
The imperative addresses only the 2nd person (أَنْتَ, أَنْتِ, etc.).
For plural masculine: add وا at end; for feminine: use يَ form.

quranicExample.english: Read in the name of your Lord who created!
quranicExample.source: Surah Al-Alaq 96:1 — اقْرَأْ is the imperative of قَرَأَ

title: Singular, Dual & Plural
description: Learn how to form dual and plural forms of nouns in Arabic, including sound plurals and broken plurals.

concepts.description: One person or thing.
Exactly two persons or things. Formed by adding ـَانِ (rafa') or ـَيْنِ (nasb/jarr) to the singular.
Three or more persons or things. Can be sound masculine plural, sound feminine plural, or broken plural.

rules: Dual: add ـَانِ in rafa' state, ـَيْنِ in nasb and jarr states.
Sound masculine plural (جمع مذكر سالم): add ـُونَ (rafa') or ـِينَ (nasb/jarr) to masculine noun.
Sound feminine plural (جمع مؤنث سالم): replace ة with ـَاتٌ (rafa') or ـَاتٍ (nasb/jarr).
Broken plural (جمع تكسير): internal vowel pattern changes — must be memorized.
Non-rational plurals (things/animals) are treated grammatically as feminine singular.

examples.english: The two students are in the school.

quranicExample.english: And the Muslim men and Muslim women
quranicExample.source: Surah Al-Ahzab 33:35 — sound masculine and feminine plurals
